heroOverview: Anorexia as a symptom (ICD-10: R63.0) refers to a significant, persistent reduction in appetite that leads to inadequate food intake and, when prolonged, to weight loss, nutritional deficiency, and muscle wasting. It is distinct from anorexia nervosa, the psychiatric eating disorder. Symptom-based anorexia is estimated to affect up to 30–50% of patients with chronic illness, and it is among the most common presenting complaints in medical oncology, where it contributes directly to cancer-related weight loss. The underlying mechanism involves disrupted appetite-regulating signals — including ghrelin, leptin, and pro-inflammatory cytokines — that override the normal drive to eat. Identifying and treating the cause, rather than the symptom alone, is the cornerstone of management.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects 30–50% of patients with advanced cancer and a high proportion of those with chronic infections, heart failure, or renal disease (WHO, 2020)
keyStats.demographics: Occurs across all ages; more common in older adults with multiple chronic diseases and in patients receiving chemotherapy or radiotherapy
keyStats.avgAge: No single peak age; prevalence rises sharply in adults over 65 and in those with life-limiting illness
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated to affect hundreds of millions as a secondary symptom of underlying illness worldwide each year

definition: Anorexia (ICD-10: R63.0) is the medical term for loss or absence of appetite, used as a clinical symptom rather than a psychiatric diagnosis. The appetite-regulating system involves a balance between orexigenic signals (ghrelin, neuropeptide Y) that stimulate eating and anorexigenic signals (leptin, pro-inflammatory cytokines such as TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6) that suppress it. In disease states, cytokine release, tumour metabolites, gut-hormone dysregulation, pain, nausea, depression, and side effects of medications all suppress the orexigenic drive. This results in reduced caloric intake, progressive weight loss, sarcopenia, and nutritional deficiency if not addressed. Anorexia as a symptom should be distinguished from anorexia nervosa (F50.0), a psychiatric disorder characterised by deliberate food restriction driven by a distorted body image. Clinically, symptom-based anorexia is managed within General Medicine, Gastroenterology, Oncology, and Geriatrics. It is rarely an isolated finding; a systematic search for the underlying cause — infection, malignancy, metabolic disorder, medications, psychiatric illness, or structural gastrointestinal disease — drives treatment planning.

typesClassification.description: Loss of appetite caused by an underlying condition such as cancer, chronic infection (HIV, tuberculosis), heart failure, or renal disease; the most common form and typically resolves or improves with treatment of the cause.
Reduced appetite as a side effect of drugs including chemotherapy, opioids, digoxin, metformin, antibiotics, and SSRIs; often reversible on dose adjustment or drug change.
Appetite suppression driven by depression, anxiety, grief, or acute stress; the gut–brain axis mediates reduced appetite signals in psychological distress.
A gradual decline in appetite occurring normally with advancing age due to reduced sensory perception, altered gut-hormone responses, slower gastric emptying, and decreased physical activity.

primarySymptoms: Diminished or absent desire to eat, persisting across multiple meals and not attributable to nausea alone.
Early satiety — feeling full after eating only a small amount, even when the last meal was hours ago.
Unintentional weight loss occurring over days to weeks in parallel with reduced food intake.
Fatigue and low energy disproportionate to activity level, reflecting inadequate caloric intake.
Reduced interest in food-related activities — shopping, cooking, or sitting at mealtimes — that the person previously enjoyed.
Muscle weakness or difficulty with tasks requiring sustained physical effort, signalling protein-energy depletion.
Pallor or dry skin associated with micronutrient deficiencies developing over weeks of inadequate intake.
Difficulty concentrating or low mood, which can both result from poor nutrition and also perpetuate reduced appetite.
Nausea or a vague sense of abdominal discomfort that discourages eating even when hunger is absent.
In children and adolescents, failure to gain expected weight or a plateau in growth velocity.
earlyWarningSigns: Skipping one or two meals a day without feeling hungry, lasting more than two to three days.
Serving noticeably smaller portions and leaving food on the plate at most meals.
Feeling 'too tired' or 'not in the mood' for meals that were previously enjoyed.
Losing 2–3 kg in two weeks without any intentional change in diet or exercise.
emergencySigns: Weight loss greater than 10% of body weight over three months, suggesting severe nutritional depletion.
Signs of dehydration: dry mouth, dark urine, sunken eyes, and dizziness on standing.
Confusion, disorientation, or extreme weakness that may indicate electrolyte imbalance or hypoglycaemia.
In a child: acute lethargy, sunken fontanelle (infants), or failure to respond normally.
Severe abdominal pain or vomiting accompanying the anorexia, which could indicate a surgical emergency.

causes.description: Tumours release pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) and tumour-derived factors that cross the blood–brain barrier and suppress the hypothalamic appetite centres, producing the anorexia–cachexia syndrome seen in 60–80% of patients with advanced cancer.

Infections including HIV, tuberculosis, hepatitis C, and bacterial endocarditis sustain cytokine release that inhibits ghrelin secretion and downregulates appetite signals in the hypothalamus, often leading to progressive weight loss even before the diagnosis is established.

A wide range of drugs suppress appetite through central or peripheral mechanisms: chemotherapy agents cause mucositis and nausea; opioids slow gastric emptying; antibiotics disrupt gut microbiota; SSRIs reduce appetite in the first weeks of use; digoxin and spironolactone cause nausea that effectively reduces intake.

Psychological distress dysregulates the gut–brain axis, reduces serotonin's orexigenic role, and blunts the normal pleasure response to food (food anhedonia), resulting in appetite loss that tracks closely with mood state and improves when the mood disorder is treated.

Hypothyroidism slows gastric motility and blunts hunger signals; hypercalcaemia (from hyperparathyroidism or malignancy) directly suppresses appetite; adrenal insufficiency reduces cortisol's orexigenic effect; uncontrolled diabetes reduces appetite through acidosis and osmotic symptoms.

Peptic ulcer disease, gastroesophageal reflux, gastroparesis, and inflammatory bowel disease cause pain, nausea, or early satiety that conditions a learned aversion to eating, effectively suppressing appetite through classical conditioning as well as direct gut-hormone disruption.

In chronic heart failure, poor gut perfusion and elevated right-heart filling pressures cause gut oedema and dysmotility; in renal failure, uraemic toxins directly suppress appetite and cause a dysgeusia that makes food taste metallic or unpleasant, both leading to substantial intake reduction.

riskFactors.description: Cancer, especially pancreatic, lung, gastric, and colorectal types, is the most common cause of clinically significant disease-related anorexia; the anorexia–cachexia syndrome affects 60–80% of patients at advanced stages.
Taking five or more medications substantially raises the probability of at least one appetite-suppressing drug; a medication review by a pharmacist or physician can often reduce this burden.
Physiological anorexia of ageing affects up to 30% of adults over 75, driven by altered hormone profiles, reduced sensory pleasure from food, slower gastric emptying, and social isolation.

Untreated mood disorders are among the most common and reversible causes of anorexia in primary care; effective treatment of the underlying disorder restores appetite in the majority of affected patients.
Uraemic toxins in renal failure and portal hypertension-related gut oedema in liver disease both directly suppress appetite and alter taste, with severity tracking disease stage.

riskFactors.factor: Radiation therapy to the head, neck, or abdomen
riskFactors.description: Radiotherapy to these regions causes mucositis, xerostomia, taste changes, and oesophagitis that make eating painful and unpleasant; supportive measures and dietary modification reduce the impact during treatment.

riskFactors.factor: Social isolation and poor food access
riskFactors.description: Living alone, food insecurity, inability to cook, and eating without company each reduce food intake independently of disease; social dining and meal delivery services demonstrably improve intake in at-risk older adults.
Uncontrolled pain suppresses appetite through opioid-system dysregulation and reduces the motivation to prepare and eat meals; optimising pain control typically improves food intake.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 65 with multiple chronic conditions, the group most frequently affected
Patients receiving cancer treatment, particularly chemotherapy or abdominal radiotherapy
People living with HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis, or other chronic infections
Individuals with moderate-to-severe depression or generalised anxiety disorder
Patients with end-stage renal or cardiac disease
Children with acute infections, where short-term anorexia is almost universal
diagnosisOverview: Evaluating anorexia begins with a thorough history to determine onset, duration, severity of weight loss, associated symptoms, and all medications. A validated screening tool — such as the Mini Nutritional Assessment (MNA) in older adults or the Patient-Generated Subjective Global Assessment (PG-SGA) in oncology — quantifies nutritional risk. Physical examination includes weight, BMI, mid-arm circumference, and signs of micronutrient deficiency. Laboratory tests are used to find the underlying cause: a full blood count looks for anaemia and infection; metabolic panel assesses renal and liver function; calcium and thyroid-stimulating hormone screen for common metabolic causes; C-reactive protein and albumin reflect inflammation and nutritional status. A chest X-ray and abdominal imaging are ordered when malignancy or visceral disease is suspected. Endoscopy evaluates the upper gastrointestinal tract when dysphagia, early satiety, or haematemesis is present. A depression and anxiety screen (PHQ-9, GAD-7) is integral, because mood disorders are among the most common treatable causes in primary care. The goal is to identify a reversible or treatable cause before attributing anorexia to disease progression alone.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count and inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for anaemia, infection, chronic inflammation, and haematological malignancy as causes of appetite loss.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw from the arm; results available within hours to one day.

diagnosticTests.test: Metabolic panel (renal function, liver enzymes, calcium, glucose)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies kidney failure, liver disease, hypercalcaemia, and diabetes — all common metabolic causes of anorexia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same blood sample as above; no fasting required for most components.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects hypothyroidism, which slows motility and blunts appetite, and hyperthyroidism, which occasionally suppresses appetite in older adults.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood test; normal range varies by lab; result available within one to two days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Pre-albumin reflects recent nutritional status (half-life 2–3 days) and helps quantify the degree of protein-energy depletion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Part of the same blood draw; albumin is also lowered by inflammation, so it is interpreted alongside CRP.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualises the oesophagus, stomach, and duodenum to detect ulcers, malignancy, gastritis, or pyloric obstruction causing early satiety.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under light sedation; takes 10–15 minutes; throat may feel mildly sore afterwards; biopsy samples may be taken.

diagnosticTests.test: CT scan of the chest and abdomen
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for occult malignancy, lymphadenopathy, and abdominal pathology when bloodwork and endoscopy are non-diagnostic.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless 10–20 minute scan; iodinated contrast is often given intravenously to improve visualisation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Validated structured tools that quantify nutritional risk and guide urgency of dietitian referral and intervention.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A questionnaire completed by the clinician and patient together; takes about 5–10 minutes; no blood draw needed.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of anorexia as a symptom depends almost entirely on identifying and managing the underlying cause. When a reversible cause is found — depression, a medication side effect, a curable infection, hypercalcaemia — treating it restores appetite in most patients. Nutritional support runs in parallel with cause-directed treatment: a registered dietitian optimises caloric density, meal frequency, and texture; oral nutritional supplements bridge the gap between intake and requirements; enteral tube feeding is used when the gut is functional but oral intake is critically inadequate. Pharmacological appetite stimulants are considered when the cause is irreversible or when nutritional decline continues despite optimal nutritional support. Megestrol acetate and dronabinol are the most evidence-based agents and can improve both appetite and caloric intake. Corticosteroids (dexamethasone, prednisolone) improve appetite short-term, mainly in palliative settings. Mirtazapine, an antidepressant with significant appetite-stimulating properties, is useful when depression co-exists. The goal is to maintain or restore adequate nutritional intake and quality of life, not simply to achieve a weight number.

medicalTreatments.name: Treatment of the underlying cause
medicalTreatments.description: Addressing the primary driver — antibiotics for infection, antidepressants for depression, calcium-lowering therapy for hypercalcaemia, thyroid replacement for hypothyroidism — is the most effective intervention and restores appetite in the majority of reversible cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Appetite returns in 60–80% of cases where the underlying cause is successfully treated, based on clinical series across primary care.

medicalTreatments.name: Megestrol acetate
medicalTreatments.description: A progestational agent that stimulates appetite through central and peripheral mechanisms, increasing caloric intake and body weight; licensed specifically for anorexia and cachexia in cancer and HIV. Carries thromboembolic risk and should be used at the lowest effective dose.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves appetite in approximately 70–75% of patients with cancer-related anorexia in randomised trials; weight gain is modest (2–3 kg on average).

medicalTreatments.name: Dronabinol (synthetic cannabinoid)
medicalTreatments.description: A synthetic tetrahydrocannabinol that stimulates CB1 receptors in the hypothalamus to increase appetite and reduce nausea; used in HIV-associated anorexia and as a second-line option in cancer-related anorexia–cachexia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves appetite scores in roughly 50–60% of HIV-related anorexia; modest weight gain compared with placebo in controlled trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroids (dexamethasone, prednisolone)
medicalTreatments.description: Short-term use in palliative care improves appetite and well-being within days; benefit typically wanes after four to eight weeks, and long-term use causes significant muscle loss, osteoporosis, and immune suppression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Produces clinically meaningful appetite improvement in about 60–80% of patients in palliative-care studies for durations of up to four weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Mirtazapine
medicalTreatments.description: A tetracyclic antidepressant whose H1 and 5-HT2C receptor blockade produces significant appetite stimulation and weight gain as side effects; particularly useful when anorexia is driven by or accompanied by depression or anxiety.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Associated with weight gain of 1–2 kg over 4–8 weeks in depressed patients with anorexia compared with other antidepressants; appetite improvement often precedes mood improvement.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral nutritional supplements (ONS)
medicalTreatments.description: High-calorie, high-protein drinks prescribed by a dietitian to bridge the gap between voluntary food intake and estimated requirements; available in a range of flavours and textures to suit individual preferences.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analyses show ONS increase total energy intake by 20–30% and reduce hospital readmission rates by about 30% in malnourished older adults.

alternativeTreatments.name: Small, frequent, calorie-dense meals
alternativeTreatments.description: Offering 5–6 small portions of nutrient-dense food per day rather than three large meals reduces the effort each eating occasion demands, improves total intake, and prevents the discouragement of repeatedly failing to finish a full plate.

alternativeTreatments.name: Appetite stimulation through smell and taste enhancement
alternativeTreatments.description: Warming food slightly above room temperature, adding aromatic herbs, and offering preferred cultural foods exploits the cephalic-phase appetite response to improve spontaneous intake in clinical trials of older adults.

alternativeTreatments.name: Social eating interventions
alternativeTreatments.description: Eating with family, friends, or in a communal dining setting has been shown in randomised trials of care-home residents to improve caloric intake by 20–25% compared with eating alone.

whySeeSpecialist: Most simple short-term appetite loss resolves with treatment of an obvious acute illness and does not require specialist referral. A specialist — gastroenterologist, oncologist, geriatrician, or palliative-care physician, depending on context — should be involved when anorexia persists for more than two to four weeks without a clear explanation, when it is accompanied by more than 5% unintentional weight loss in one month, when malignancy cannot be excluded, or when nutritional depletion is severe enough to affect functional capacity. A registered dietitian is a core team member for any patient with ongoing inadequate intake.
doctorSelectionGuide: A general practitioner handles initial evaluation and management of most acute causes. A gastroenterologist is appropriate when upper GI pathology is suspected. For oncology-related anorexia, a specialist oncology dietitian and palliative-care team provide the best multidisciplinary support. In older adults with multiple contributing factors, a geriatrician or a dedicated nutrition support team offers the most comprehensive approach. Ask whether the practice uses validated nutritional screening tools and has dietitian access within the clinic.
preventionStrategies: Review and rationalise medications regularly, particularly in older adults, to identify and eliminate appetite-suppressing drugs where alternatives exist.
Screen for and treat depression and anxiety promptly, since mood disorders are among the most common reversible causes of appetite loss in all age groups.
Maintain adequate physical activity, which preserves appetite-stimulating signals through muscle-derived hormones and improves gastric motility.
Ensure adequate social connections and shared mealtimes, which are independent determinants of food intake in older adults.
For patients beginning cancer treatment, involve a dietitian before therapy starts to establish nutritional goals and anticipate treatment-related anorexia.
Monitor weight regularly in patients with chronic disease, since a 5% loss over one month is the clinical threshold for nutritional intervention.
lifestyleModifications: Eat small portions every 2–3 hours rather than waiting for hunger to return.
Choose calorie-dense foods — nuts, nut butters, avocado, full-fat dairy, eggs — so each mouthful delivers more energy.
Eat with others when possible; social dining reliably improves intake.
Reduce fluid intake with meals to avoid filling the stomach before food.
Identify preferred flavours and textures and keep ready-to-eat versions available.
Keep a simple food diary to show a dietitian; recording intake improves awareness and reveals patterns.
Manage pain, nausea, and fatigue before mealtimes where possible, as these are the most common immediate barriers to eating.

recommended: Energy-dense, nutrient-rich foods: whole eggs, full-fat yoghurt, cheese, oily fish, avocado, nut butters
Fortified foods and oral nutritional supplements prescribed by a dietitian
Smoothies and soups that pack protein and calories into an easily consumed volume
Preferred cultural foods that hold positive associations and stimulate appetite through familiarity
avoid: Large volumes of plain water immediately before or during meals, which displaces food
Low-calorie diet foods or 'light' versions of products when weight maintenance is the goal
Skipping meals entirely; even small amounts consumed regularly maintain metabolic function
Foods that the patient finds nauseating or aversive, even if normally nutritious

exerciseGuidelines: Light to moderate exercise — short walks, gentle resistance exercises with bands or body weight — can modestly stimulate appetite by increasing ghrelin release and improving gastric motility. Exercise should be tailored to the individual's functional capacity, particularly in those with severe weight loss or underlying illness. For frail patients, physiotherapy-led graded exercise programmes are preferred over self-directed activity. Avoid strenuous exercise that exceeds caloric intake capacity, which would worsen negative energy balance.
dailyManagement: Set reminders or alarms to eat every 2–3 hours, as hunger cues may be absent.
Prepare or store small calorie-dense snacks in easily accessible locations.
Weigh once weekly at the same time and record results to track trends.
Report any weight loss greater than 1 kg per week or worsening weakness to a clinician promptly.
Take prescribed appetite stimulants or nutritional supplements consistently, not only on days when intake feels poor.
Communicate openly with the healthcare team about barriers to eating, including nausea, pain, or depression.
prognosis: The prognosis of anorexia as a symptom depends almost entirely on the underlying cause. When the cause is reversible — depression, a medication side effect, an acute infection, or a treatable metabolic disorder — appetite and weight typically recover fully within weeks to months of effective treatment, and long-term outcome is excellent. In chronic conditions such as heart failure, renal disease, or HIV, anorexia fluctuates with disease activity and can be managed with nutritional support and, where indicated, appetite stimulants. In advanced cancer, anorexia–cachexia syndrome is common, only partially responsive to pharmacological treatment, and is associated with shorter survival, though aggressive nutritional support and multimodal care improve quality of life and functional status. In older adults with physiological anorexia of ageing, structured nutritional interventions and social support slow the trajectory of weight loss and reduce hospitalisations.
recoveryTimeline: When a reversible cause is identified and treated, appetite typically begins to return within one to two weeks and reaches prior baseline within four to six weeks in uncomplicated cases. Nutritional deficits accumulated over months may take longer to correct, particularly lean muscle mass, which rebuilds slowly. In oncology patients receiving treatment, appetite typically improves between treatment cycles but may not fully recover until treatment ends. Patients losing 5% or more of body weight over one month warrant urgent dietitian assessment regardless of the trajectory.
complications: Protein-energy malnutrition leading to muscle wasting, impaired wound healing, and reduced immune function.
Micronutrient deficiencies — particularly iron, zinc, vitamin B12, folate, and vitamin D — causing anaemia, immune dysfunction, and bone loss.
Sarcopenia and frailty, which increase fall risk, hospital admission rates, and 12-month mortality in older adults.
Delayed surgical recovery and increased postoperative complication rates in malnourished patients.
Worsening of the underlying disease, since many conditions (cancer, heart failure, infections) are exacerbated by nutritional depletion.
Psychosocial consequences: anxiety around mealtimes, social withdrawal, and carer burden can perpetuate the cycle of reduced intake.

supportResources.name: NIH — National Institute on Aging: Healthy Eating After 60
supportResources.description: Evidence-based guidance on maintaining adequate nutrition and recognising appetite changes in older adults.

supportResources.name: BAPEN — Malnutrition Universal Screening Tool (MUST)
supportResources.description: Free validated tool and guidance for identifying malnutrition risk in adults, widely used in UK clinical settings.

supportResources.name: Cancer Research UK — Eating Problems During Treatment
supportResources.description: Practical advice on managing eating difficulties during cancer treatment.

sources.title: Laviano A et al. Anorexia of aging: a modifiable risk factor for frailty. Nutrients, 2021
Fearon K et al. Definition and classification of cancer cachexia: an international consensus. Lancet Oncology, 2011
Morley JE. Anorexia of ageing: a true geriatric syndrome. Journal of Nutrition, Health & Aging, 2012
NICE NG22 — Nutrition support for adults: oral nutrition support, enteral tube feeding and parenteral nutrition
Arends J et al. ESPEN guidelines on nutrition in cancer patients. Clinical Nutrition, 2017
Neelemaat F et al. Short-term oral nutritional intervention with protein and vitamin D decreases falls in malnourished older adults. JAGS, 2012

confusedWithConditions.name: Anorexia nervosa
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anorexia nervosa is a psychiatric disorder characterised by deliberate food restriction driven by a distorted body image; symptom-based anorexia (R63.0) is appetite loss due to medical illness, medication, or psychological distress without intentional restriction.

confusedWithConditions.name: Depression
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Depression can cause appetite loss as a symptom, but depression is a distinct diagnosis requiring specific psychological or pharmacological treatment beyond nutritional support alone.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cachexia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cachexia is a complex metabolic syndrome causing involuntary weight loss and muscle wasting driven by inflammation; anorexia (R63.0) refers specifically to the appetite component and may or may not progress to cachexia.

relatedConditions.name: Cachexia
relatedConditions.relevance: Cachexia is the metabolic syndrome of involuntary weight loss and muscle wasting that often follows prolonged anorexia in chronic disease.

relatedConditions.name: Underweight
relatedConditions.relevance: Underweight is a frequent outcome of prolonged anorexia when intake has been insufficient to maintain body weight.

relatedConditions.name: Anorexia nervosa
relatedConditions.relevance: The psychiatric counterpart of symptom-based anorexia; distinguished by intentional restriction and body-image disturbance.

relatedConditions.name: Depression
relatedConditions.relevance: Depression is both a major cause and a frequent consequence of persistent anorexia and nutritional depletion.

faqs.question: What is the difference between anorexia (R63.0) and anorexia nervosa?
faqs.answer: Anorexia (R63.0) is a medical symptom — loss of appetite due to illness, medication, or psychological distress. Anorexia nervosa is a psychiatric disorder defined by deliberate food restriction driven by fear of weight gain and a distorted body image. The two are different diagnoses with different treatments.

faqs.question: What causes sudden loss of appetite in adults?
faqs.answer: Common causes include acute infections, new medications, depression, stress, and gastrointestinal conditions such as gastritis or peptic ulcer. Persistent appetite loss beyond two weeks, especially with weight loss, needs medical evaluation to exclude serious underlying disease.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for loss of appetite?
faqs.answer: Seek medical attention if appetite loss has persisted for more than two weeks, if you have lost more than 5% of your body weight unintentionally in one month, or if loss of appetite is accompanied by fatigue, pain, fever, or difficulty swallowing.

faqs.question: Can depression cause loss of appetite?
faqs.answer: Yes. Depression is one of the most common causes of appetite loss. It disrupts gut-brain signalling and reduces the pleasure response to food. Treating depression with antidepressants or psychotherapy typically restores appetite alongside mood improvement.

faqs.question: How is anorexia as a symptom treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment focuses on identifying and correcting the underlying cause. Nutritional support, high-calorie foods, and oral supplements maintain intake while investigations proceed. Appetite stimulants such as megestrol acetate or mirtazapine are used when the cause is not reversible.

faqs.question: What medications cause loss of appetite?
faqs.answer: Many drugs suppress appetite, including chemotherapy agents, opioids, antibiotics, SSRIs, digoxin, metformin, and spironolactone. A medication review by a clinician can often identify and substitute or adjust the responsible drug.

faqs.question: Is loss of appetite normal in older adults?
faqs.answer: A gradual reduction in appetite is common with ageing due to hormonal changes and slower gastric emptying. However, significant or rapid appetite loss and unintentional weight loss are never simply 'normal ageing' and should be assessed for a treatable underlying cause.

faqs.question: Can cancer cause loss of appetite?
faqs.answer: Yes. Tumours release cytokines that suppress appetite-regulating centres in the brain. Anorexia affects 60–80% of patients with advanced cancer and is a core component of cancer-cachexia syndrome, which worsens prognosis and quality of life.

faqs.question: How do appetite stimulants work?
faqs.answer: Megestrol acetate acts on hypothalamic progesterone receptors to increase appetite. Dronabinol stimulates cannabinoid receptors to reduce nausea and increase appetite. Mirtazapine blocks histamine and serotonin receptors, producing appetite stimulation as a clinical effect.

faqs.question: What are the best foods to eat when you have no appetite?
faqs.answer: Choose small portions of calorie-dense foods: eggs, nut butters, full-fat dairy, avocado, oily fish, and fortified oral supplement drinks. Eating every 2–3 hours, regardless of hunger, helps maintain intake and prevents the energy deficit from compounding.

faqs.question: Can loss of appetite be a sign of something serious?
faqs.answer: It can. Persistent appetite loss is associated with cancer, heart failure, renal disease, liver disease, HIV, and other serious conditions. Any appetite loss lasting more than two to four weeks or accompanied by unintentional weight loss needs prompt medical evaluation.

faqs.question: How does the anorexia–cachexia syndrome differ from simple anorexia?
faqs.answer: Simple anorexia refers to loss of appetite from any cause. Cachexia is a complex metabolic syndrome, most common in cancer, where systemic inflammation drives muscle and fat loss beyond what reduced intake alone can explain. The two often coexist but require different interventions.

faqs.question: Does stress cause loss of appetite?
faqs.answer: Acute stress activates the hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal axis, temporarily suppressing appetite-stimulating hormones and increasing appetite-suppressing signals. Short-term stress-related anorexia is common and usually self-limiting once the stressor resolves.

faqs.question: What blood tests are done for unexplained appetite loss?
faqs.answer: Standard tests include a full blood count, kidney and liver function, calcium, thyroid-stimulating hormone, CRP, albumin, and blood glucose. These screen for the most common medical causes of appetite loss and help guide further investigation.

faqs.question: Can anorexia as a symptom be prevented?
faqs.answer: Prevention involves managing chronic disease effectively, reviewing medications regularly to eliminate appetite-suppressing drugs, treating depression promptly, maintaining physical activity, and ensuring adequate social support around mealtimes.

faqs.question: Does hypothyroidism cause loss of appetite?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hypothyroidism slows gastric motility and blunts hunger signals, leading to reduced food intake. It also causes fatigue and depression, which further suppress appetite. Thyroid replacement therapy corrects both the metabolic and appetite abnormalities.

faqs.question: How much weight loss from anorexia is medically significant?
faqs.answer: A loss of 5% or more of body weight within one month, or 10% or more within six months, is clinically significant and warrants urgent nutritional assessment and investigation for an underlying cause, regardless of starting weight.

faqs.question: Can nutritional supplements replace meals when appetite is poor?
faqs.answer: Oral nutritional supplements can effectively bridge the caloric gap between voluntary food intake and energy requirements, and they reduce hospital readmissions in malnourished patients. They work best as additions to food rather than complete meal replacements, and use should be guided by a dietitian.

faqs.question: Is anorexia in cancer patients treatable?
faqs.answer: Yes, partially. Megestrol acetate and corticosteroids improve appetite scores in most patients with cancer-related anorexia and can increase caloric intake. Nutritional support improves quality of life and functional status. However, the metabolic component of cancer cachexia does not fully respond to any currently available pharmacological treatment.

faqs.question: What role does a dietitian play in managing anorexia?
faqs.answer: A registered dietitian assesses nutritional status, estimates requirements, designs eating plans tailored to preferences and tolerances, prescribes oral supplements when needed, and monitors progress over time. Dietitian involvement significantly improves outcomes in hospitalised and community patients with disease-related anorexia.

metaTitle: Anorexia (Loss of Appetite): Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Anorexia (R63.0) is persistent appetite loss caused by illness, medications, or depression. Learn symptoms, causes, diagnosis, and treatments. Doctor-reviewed.
